title: Ansible: A Question Mark to a Therefore Symbol
content:
After more than a year of playing with AI-assisted coding, the interesting part for me is no longer "can the model write this?" but "what's the structure and process that actually makes this work?"

This project - project Ansible - is to date the most complex thing I've built. You may say I have over engineered it, but I did specifically want to try on a project that put my [agent workflow and project "template"](https://github.com/mannepanne/useful-assets-template) to the test.

## What it does

Ansible gives you AI-powered summaries of your Readwise library. You save articles faster than you read them. Your reading list grows by hundreds while the cognitive load of building cruft only grows. You need triage, not another productivity system.

Ansible syncs your unread items (articles, PDFs, videos that Reader beautifully provides transcripts of), generates summaries tagged with topics, and helps you decide: get the key takeaways and move on, or switch to Reader for the full deal. It's a two-tier reading system: summary-first consumption, selective deep-dive.

Live at: https://ansible.hultberg.org

## How it was built

Ansible is the first project built entirely with my "templated" workflow:

  - 240 tests, 95%+ coverage - Written first, guiding development
  - 6 implementation phases - Each with clear scope and acceptance criteria
  - Lifecycle-based documentation - Specifications in SPECIFICATIONS/ for active work, moved to ARCHIVE/ when complete, implementation details in REFERENCE/
  - Agent team PR reviews - Multiple AI perspectives catching issues
  - Full traceability - From original idea to implementation, every decision documented

The stack: Next.js, React, Cloudflare Workers, Perplexity AI, Supabase. TypeScript throughout. Clean architecture, proper error handling, WCAG accessibility.

## The collaboration

Working with Claude Code is a joy. It's just not a code generator - we're thinking through problems together, designing systems, making architectural trade-offs.

The main focus is this: stop rushing to code. Spend time on specifications. Make the agent write tests first. Use AI agents to review your work from multiple angles. Make the agent document as you go, and focus on ensuring that the tests are maintained, the documentation updated, and that the outcome matches expectations.

It feels slower at first. But you end up with something you can come back to six months later and actually understand. Something you can tell the agent to extend without it breaking. Something that works and evolves.

But here's what I find really surprising: it feels like drawing. Or playing with play-do.

Working this way with specs, tests, and the agents catching mistakes through PR reviews, I'm not "programming". I'm sketching. The tests are guardrails that let me experiment and shape things. The PR reviews catch issues. The GitHub log shows the journey.

I can try things, reshape them, follow an idea to see where it goes. It's the same feeling I had doing free-form pencil drawing as a kid: fluid, creative, playful. The built in constraints make me braver, not more careful.

That's the real unlock. Not that AI can write code, but that a considered way of working turns AI-assisted coding into something more like painting.
author: Magnus Hultberg
